name: TIFO matchday pack — football basics
note: Curated, factual football knowledge grounded in the IFAB Laws of the Game and standard tournament practice. Used as the retrieval corpus for the offline companion. Keep each entry self-contained and verifiable; do not add speculation.
source: IFAB Laws of the Game (theifab.com/laws/latest) + standard competition rules

entries.text: A player is in an offside position if any part of the head, body or feet is in the opponents' half and nearer to the opponents' goal line than both the ball and the second-last opponent. The hands and arms are not counted, since they cannot legally score.

Being in an offside position is not an offence by itself. It is only penalised if, at the moment a teammate plays the ball, that player becomes involved in active play by interfering with play, interfering with an opponent, or gaining an advantage from being in that position.

There is no offside offence if a player receives the ball directly from a goal kick, a corner kick, or a throw-in. A player level with the second-last opponent or level with the last two opponents is considered onside.

It is an offence if a player deliberately touches the ball with the hand or arm, or scores with the hand or arm, or makes their body unnaturally bigger with the arm. The arm is judged from the bottom of the armpit down. Accidental handball by an attacker is only penalised if it leads immediately to a goal or a goal-scoring chance.

A direct free kick is awarded for offences such as kicking, tripping, charging, pushing, holding, or handball. From a direct free kick a goal can be scored directly against the opponents without anyone else touching the ball.

An indirect free kick is awarded for offences such as dangerous play, impeding an opponent without contact, or a goalkeeper holding the ball too long. A goal can only be scored from an indirect free kick if the ball touches another player before going in. The referee signals it by raising one arm.

A penalty kick is awarded when a player commits a direct-free-kick offence inside their own penalty area while the ball is in play. It is taken from the penalty mark, twelve yards (about eleven metres) from goal, with only the goalkeeper to beat.
A yellow card is a caution shown for offences such as unsporting behaviour, dissent, persistent fouling, delaying the restart of play, or entering or leaving the field without permission.

A red card sends a player off and the team continues with one fewer player. It is shown for serious foul play, violent conduct, spitting, denying an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by handball or by a foul, offensive language, or receiving a second yellow card in the same match.
A player who receives two yellow cards in the same match is shown a red card and sent off. The team must then play with one fewer player for the rest of the match.

A match is two halves of forty-five minutes each, with a half-time interval of no more than fifteen minutes. The referee adds stoppage time at the end of each half to make up for time lost to substitutions, injuries, and other delays.

Most major competitions allow each team up to five substitutions, taken in up to three stoppages during play plus at half-time. Permanent concussion substitutes are a separate, additional option where the competition has adopted the protocol.

In knockout matches that are level after ninety minutes, some competitions play extra time of two halves of fifteen minutes each. There is no longer a golden-goal rule; both periods are played in full.

If a knockout tie is still level after extra time, the winner is decided by a penalty shootout. Teams take five kicks each, alternating, and if still level after five it continues into sudden death, one pair of kicks at a time, until one team scores and the other misses.

VAR can review only four match-changing situations: goals and any offence in the build-up to them, penalty decisions, direct red cards (but not second yellow cards), and mistaken identity when the wrong player is cautioned or sent off. VAR only recommends a review for a clear and obvious error, and the on-field referee makes the final decision, often after watching the pitchside monitor.

A goal is scored when the whole of the ball passes over the goal line, between the goalposts and under the crossbar, provided no offence was committed by the scoring team. The team that scores more goals wins; if both score the same number, or none, the match is drawn.

A corner kick is awarded to the attacking team when the whole ball passes over the goal line, outside the goal, having last touched a defending player. It is taken from the corner arc nearest where the ball went out, and a goal can be scored directly from it.

A goal kick is awarded to the defending team when the whole ball passes over the goal line, outside the goal, having last touched an attacker. The ball is in play as soon as it is kicked and clearly moves, and opponents must be outside the penalty area until then.

A throw-in restarts play when the whole ball crosses a touchline. The thrower must face the field, keep both feet on or behind the line, and deliver the ball with both hands from behind and over the head. A goal cannot be scored directly from a throw-in.
Kick-off starts each half and restarts play after a goal. The ball may be played in any direction from the centre mark, and a goal can be scored directly from kick-off against the opponents.

A dropped ball restarts play after a stoppage that was not caused by an offence, for example when play is halted for an injury. It is dropped for one player of the team that last touched the ball, and everyone else must stay at least four metres away until it touches the ground.

The goalkeeper may handle the ball only inside their own penalty area. They cannot handle a ball deliberately kicked to them by a teammate, nor a teammate's throw-in, and must release the ball into play within about six seconds of controlling it with the hands.

The referee may apply advantage and let play continue when the team that was fouled would benefit from doing so. If the expected advantage does not develop within a few seconds, the referee can return and award the original free kick.
The penalty area is the large box in front of each goal. Inside it the defending goalkeeper may handle the ball, and a direct-free-kick foul by the defending team there results in a penalty kick.

Matches are played on a rectangular pitch marked with a halfway line and centre circle, two penalty areas, two goal areas, and a goal at each end. The two longer boundary lines are the touchlines and the two shorter ones are the goal lines.

entries.topic: The ball in and out of play
entries.text: The ball is out of play when it has wholly crossed a goal line or touchline on the ground or in the air, or when the referee stops play. Otherwise it remains in play, including when it rebounds off a goalpost, crossbar, corner flag, or the referee and stays on the field.
Each team has eleven players on the field, one of whom is the goalkeeper. A match may not start or continue if either team has fewer than seven players.
The captain is a designated player who represents the team but has no special status under the Laws beyond a degree of responsibility for the conduct of the team.

At a free kick, opponents must stay at least ten yards (about nine metres) from the ball until it is in play, unless they are on their own goal line between the posts. When three or more defenders form a wall, attackers must stay at least one metre away from it.

In hot conditions competitions may schedule cooling breaks of up to three minutes, usually around the halfway point of each half. They are distinct from shorter drinks breaks and the time is added on as stoppage time.

Many tournaments begin with a group stage in which teams play each other and earn three points for a win, one for a draw, and none for a loss. The top teams in each group advance to the knockout rounds.

In the knockout stage a match must produce a winner, so a tie level after normal time goes to extra time and, if needed, a penalty shootout. Losing a knockout match eliminates the team from the competition.

Two-legged knockout ties are decided on aggregate, the total score across both matches. Many competitions have abolished the away-goals rule, so a tie level on aggregate goes to extra time and then penalties rather than being settled by goals scored away.

Stoppage time, also called added or injury time, is the extra time the referee adds at the end of each half to compensate for time lost during the half. The fourth official typically signals the minimum amount to be played.
